past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, managed with oral hypoglycemics and insulin
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 8 years ago, on levothyroxine replacement
Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well controlled on metoprolol
Bariatric surgery (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass) 7 years ago
Depression, treated with fluoxetine for 3 years
family_history: Mother had essential tremor, diagnosed at age 60, died at 84 from pneumonia
Father had type 2 diabetes and hypertension, died at age 76 from stroke
One brother, age 72, with type 2 diabetes and mild cognitive impairment
No known family history of Parkinson's disease or other neurodegenerative disorders

social_history.alcohol: Rare, approximately one glass of wine per month at social gatherings
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a single-story condominium; daughter lives nearby and visits several times per week
social_history.exercise: Previously active with daily walks of 2-3 miles and weekly yoga classes, but has curtailed both over the past 4 months due to unsteadiness and fear of falling
social_history.hobbies: Avid reader and writer; has been working on a memoir but reports increasing difficulty with handwriting over recent months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is mildly hypophonic with reduced prosody but remains fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Montreal Cognitive Assessment score 24/30, with points lost on trail-making (1 point), delayed recall (2 points), and clock drawing (1 point). Affect is mildly blunted. No evidence of hallucinations or delusions on questioning.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light (3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Marked hypomimia with reduced spontaneous blinking rate. Facial movements symmetric but diminished in amplitude. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Marked bradykinesia on finger tapping, hand opening-closing, and pronation-supination bilaterally, right worse than left. Cogwheel rigidity at both wrists, more prominent on the right. Mild rigidity at bilateral elbows, augmented with contralateral activation. Lower extremity tone mildly increased bilaterally. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No pyramidal signs.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all extremities. Proprioception intact at bilateral great toes. Vibration sense mildly reduced at bilateral ankles, likely age-related. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements reveal progressive decrement in speed and amplitude bilaterally, right greater than left, consistent with bradykinesia rather than cerebellar dysfunction. Heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without ataxia.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait notable for wide base, stooped posture, and bilateral reduction in arm swing with the right arm nearly immobile during ambulation. Shortened stride length with mild shuffling. Turns require 5-6 steps (en bloc turning). Positive pull test with retropulsion of 2 steps but self-corrects. No festination observed. Tandem gait impaired. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Bilateral jerky resting tremor present in both hands, right greater than left, at approximately 4-5 Hz, with a pill-rolling quality. Tremor also present with posture holding in both hands after a brief re-emergent latency. Tremor suppressed with intentional movement. No action tremor. No dystonia or chorea. Handwriting sample demonstrates progressive micrographia. Glabellar tap sign positive with failure to habituate.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive tremor in both hands, slowed movements, and multiple falls over the past 4 months
patient.history_present_illness:
Mrs. Delgado is a 68-year-old right-handed retired university professor who presents to the outpatient neurology clinic for evaluation of progressive vertigo, slowed movements, and tremor over the past 4 months. She was referred by her primary care physician after reporting a progressive worsening of motor symptoms that have begun to interfere with her daily activities and independence.

Approximately 4 months ago, the patient first noticed a sensation of unsteadiness and vertigo when turning quickly or rising from a seated position. She initially attributed this to her blood pressure medications but did not improve after her primary care physician adjusted the metoprolol dose. Around the same time, her daughter observed that the patient appeared to be moving more slowly than usual, taking longer to complete routine tasks such as dressing, cooking, and writing.

Three months ago, the patient developed a noticeable resting tremor in her right hand. The tremor was initially intermittent, appearing primarily when she was seated and relaxed, and would diminish when she reached for objects. Over the subsequent weeks, a similar but milder tremor appeared in the left hand. During this period, she has experienced three falls, all occurring when she lost her balance while turning or attempting to navigate around furniture. She did not lose consciousness during any of these episodes and denies preceding lightheadedness, palpitations, or visual changes. She sustained a minor bruise on her hip during one fall but did not fracture any bones.

The patient also reports significant difficulty with handwriting over the past 2-3 months. She has been working on a memoir and notes that her handwriting has become progressively smaller and more cramped, to the point where she can barely read her own notes. She has switched to typing on a computer but finds that her fingers feel stiff and clumsy on the keyboard as well. Buttoning clothes and fastening jewelry have become increasingly difficult, particularly with the right hand.

Additionally, she reports insomnia with difficulty initiating sleep over the past several months, which she attributes to her depressed mood. Her daughter has noticed that her mother's voice has become softer and that she seems less emotionally expressive, with a more mask-like facial appearance. The patient acknowledges feeling depressed and reports a general decline in her memory, particularly for recent events, though she continues to manage her finances and medications independently.

She reports urinary frequency and occasional urgency but denies frank incontinence. She denies hallucinations, vivid dreams, or acting out dreams during sleep. She denies difficulty swallowing or excessive drooling. She denies any exposure to antipsychotic medications, metoclopramide, or other dopamine-blocking agents. There has been no history of encephalitis, significant head trauma, or exposure to environmental toxins.

findings.location: Bilateral cerebral hemispheres, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal gray-white matter differentiation. No focal signal abnormalities in the basal ganglia, substantia nigra, or brainstem nuclei.
signal_characteristics.T2: No focal or confluent signal abnormalities. Normal signal intensity in the putamen and caudate bilaterally. No putaminal rim sign.
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Scattered non-specific periventricular and subcortical white matter hyperintensities, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease in the setting of hypertension and diabetes.
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and deep subcortical white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Multiple punctate and small confluent white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular regions and deep white matter, Fazekas grade 1-2, consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic changes.

additional_observations: No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy; Evans index 0.27 (normal <0.30)
No basal ganglia infarcts or calcifications
No features of normal pressure hydrocephalus
No cerebellar or pontine atrophy; no hot cross bun sign
Normal midbrain morphology; no hummingbird sign; normal midbrain-to-pons ratio
No evidence of mesial temporal sclerosis
mri.impression: No cerebellar or pontine atrophy to suggest MSA. No midbrain atrophy or hummingbird sign to suggest PSP. No structural cause of parkinsonism identified. No ventriculomegaly to suggest NPH. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Bilateral striatum, asymmetric with greater reduction on the left (contralateral to clinically more affected right side)
findings.signal: left_putamen: Markedly reduced tracer uptake with near-complete loss of posterior putaminal signal, producing a period-shaped (dot-like) pattern rather than the normal comma shape; left_caudate: Mildly reduced tracer uptake relative to age-matched controls; right_putamen: Moderately reduced tracer uptake in the posterior putamen with relative preservation of anterior putamen; right_caudate: Low-normal tracer uptake

output.impression: Abnormal DaTscan demonstrating bilateral asymmetric reduction in striatal dopamine transporter uptake, left greater than right, with a posterior-to-anterior gradient of putaminal involvement. The findings exclude essential tremor and drug-induced parkinsonism, in which DaTscan would be normal. Clinical correlation recommended.

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.value: Mild nausea, transient lightheadedness on standing; no dyskinesia

findings.test: Tilt table test - standing BP (3 minutes)
Heart rate variability with deep breathing

findings.value: Normal sweat volumes at all four sites

quantitative_data.QSART (quantitative sudomotor axon refle: Normal sweat volumes at all four sites

output.impression: Abnormal values: Tilt table test - standing BP (3 minutes) 122/70 mmHg mmHg (H).

Abnormal values: Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) 24 points (L), Processing Speed Index 80 standard score (L), Executive Function (Trail Making B) T-score 37 T-score (H), Verbal Fluency (FAS) 28 total words (L).

findings.value: No loss of REM atonia detected; no dream enactment behaviors observed

quantitative_data.REM sleep behavior disorder screening: No loss of REM atonia detected; no dream enactment behaviors observed

output.impression: Abnormal values: Total sleep time 5.2 hours hours (H), Sleep efficiency 72% % (H), Sleep onset latency 38 minutes minutes (H), Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) 6.2 events/hour (H), Periodic Limb Movement Index (PLMI) 18 events/hour (H).

output.proposed: Carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 mg TID for Parkinson's disease
interactions: Fluoxetine + Carbidopa-levodopa: Minor interaction. SSRIs may theoretically increase serotonergic effects when combined with levodopa, but this combination is commonly used safely in clinical practice. Monitor for serotonin-related adverse effects (agitation, tremor, myoclonus).
Metoprolol + Carbidopa-levodopa: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. Both may lower blood pressure; monitor for orthostatic hypotension.
contraindications: Non-selective MAO inhibitors are contraindicated with levodopa (risk of hypertensive crisis). Patient is not on any MAO inhibitors.
warnings: Monitor for orthostatic hypotension, especially given patient's borderline orthostatic BP drop and concurrent metoprolol and insulin use
Levodopa may worsen or unmask dyskinesia over time; start at low dose and titrate gradually
Protein-rich meals can reduce levodopa absorption; advise dosing 30 minutes before meals
Metformin does not interact with levodopa but post-bariatric surgery anatomy may affect oral absorption of all medications
alternatives: Pramipexole (dopamine agonist) - alternative first-line, may be preferred in younger patients to delay levodopa dyskinesia, but higher risk of impulse control disorders and somnolence
Ropinirole (dopamine agonist) - similar profile to pramipexole
Rasagiline (MAO-B inhibitor) - mild symptomatic benefit, may have disease-modifying potential

output.query: Parkinson's disease self-mutilation dopamine deficiency behavioral disturbance

results.title: Self-injurious behavior in movement disorders: a systematic review
results.summary: Self-injurious behavior (SIB) is well-characterized in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (complete HPRT deficiency) but has been reported rarely in idiopathic Parkinson's disease, typically associated with dopamine dysregulation syndrome or impulse control disorders from dopaminergic medications.

results.title: Impulse control disorders in Parkinson's disease: pathophysiology, risk factors, and treatment
results.summary: Impulse control disorders including pathological gambling, hypersexuality, compulsive shopping, and binge eating affect 13-30% of PD patients treated with dopamine agonists. Self-injury is rare but has been reported. Risk factors include younger age, male sex, depression, and use of dopamine agonists.

results.title: Behavioral and psychiatric features in Parkinson's disease: clinical spectrum and management
results.summary: Non-motor symptoms including depression, anxiety, apathy, and behavioral disturbances are increasingly recognized as integral features of PD rather than comorbidities. Depression occurs in 40-50% of PD patients and may precede motor symptoms by years.

output.summary: Self-injurious behavior in the context of dopamine deficiency is classically associated with Lesch-Nyhan syndrome but is distinctly rare in idiopathic Parkinson's disease. When seen in PD, it is more commonly related to dopamine dysregulation syndrome or impulse control disorders from dopaminergic therapy rather than the disease itself. Depression and other behavioral disturbances are common non-motor features of PD and are managed with appropriate psychiatric care alongside dopaminergic treatment.

output.query: atypical parkinsonism vs idiopathic Parkinson's disease differential diagnosis red flags

results.title: Red flags for atypical parkinsonian syndromes: MDS clinical diagnostic criteria
results.summary: Key red flags for atypical parkinsonism include: early and severe autonomic failure (MSA), vertical gaze palsy and early falls (PSP), alien limb and cortical sensory loss (CBD), early prominent dementia and visual hallucinations (DLB), poor or absent levodopa response, rapid progression, cerebellar ataxia, and symmetric onset.

results.title: MDS Clinical Criteria for Parkinson's Disease: updated 2023
results.summary: Supportive criteria for PD diagnosis include: clear and dramatic beneficial response to dopaminergic therapy, presence of levodopa-induced dyskinesias, rest tremor of a limb, olfactory loss, and cardiac sympathetic denervation on MIBG scintigraphy. Absolute exclusion criteria include cerebellar abnormalities, supranuclear vertical gaze palsy, and frontotemporal dementia.

results.title: Differential diagnosis of parkinsonism: a practical clinical approach
results.summary: The key distinguishing features of idiopathic PD from atypical syndromes are: asymmetric onset, resting tremor, good levodopa response, and slow progression. MSA is distinguished by early severe autonomic failure and cerebellar/pyramidal signs. PSP by supranuclear gaze palsy, axial rigidity, and early falls. CBD by asymmetric cortical signs.

output.summary: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease is distinguished from atypical parkinsonian syndromes by asymmetric onset, prominent resting tremor, robust levodopa responsiveness, and the absence of red-flag features. The most important red flags for atypical parkinsonism include early severe autonomic failure (MSA), supranuclear vertical gaze palsy (PSP), alien limb phenomenon (CBD), and early prominent dementia with visual hallucinations (DLB). A positive levodopa challenge test (>30% improvement in UPDRS-III) is the strongest supportive criterion for idiopathic PD.

differential.key_distinguishing: Early and severe autonomic failure, cerebellar signs, poor levodopa response, and rapid progression would be expected in MSA. This patient has only borderline orthostatic hypotension attributable to medications, no cerebellar signs, and no pontine or cerebellar atrophy on MRI.

Vertical gaze palsy, early falls, prominent axial rigidity greater than limb rigidity, and poor levodopa response characterize PSP. This patient has no vertical gaze palsy, no midbrain atrophy, and her falls are more consistent with postural instability in PD.

Would present with symmetric parkinsonism and is typically associated with dopamine-blocking agents (antipsychotics, metoclopramide). Patient is not on any dopamine-blocking medications. Fluoxetine rarely causes mild extrapyramidal symptoms but would not explain the full syndrome.

Typically presents with lower-body predominant gait difficulty, symmetric findings, and step-wise progression. MRI would show strategic basal ganglia infarcts. This patient's MRI shows only non-specific white matter disease without strategic infarcts.

Classic triad of gait apraxia (magnetic gait), urinary incontinence, and dementia. MRI shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy. This patient's ventricles are normal and her gait pattern is parkinsonian rather than apractic.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural causes of parkinsonism (vascular parkinsonism, NPH, space-occupying lesion) and evaluate for features of atypical parkinsonism (cerebellar/pontine atrophy in MSA, midbrain atrophy in PSP)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI for age with non-specific white matter changes; no structural cause of parkinsonism; no features of MSA or PSP

optimal_actions.action: Order laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function tests, vitamin B12, RPR, and ceruloplasmin to exclude metabolic and infectious causes of parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal lab values except for glucose/HbA1c reflecting known diabetes; no metabolic cause of parkinsonism identified

optimal_actions.action: Request DaTscan (dopamine transporter SPECT) to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit and support clinical diagnosis of Parkinson's disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced striatal dopamine transporter uptake, asymmetric, consistent with PD

optimal_actions.action: Perform levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness and support diagnosis of idiopathic PD versus atypical parkinsonism
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Robust improvement (>30%) in UPDRS Part III motor score, supporting idiopathic PD

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on differential diagnosis of parkinsonism and red flags for atypical parkinsonian syndromes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Key distinguishing features of PD versus MSA, PSP, CBD, and DLB

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating carbidopa-levodopa therapy, given patient's multiple medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No major contraindications; minor interactions noted with monitoring recommendations

optimal_actions.action: Request autonomic testing to evaluate for autonomic failure and differentiate from MSA if clinical suspicion warrants
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline orthostatic hypotension likely medication-related; no severe autonomic failure to suggest MSA

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural and secondary causes of parkinsonism
Order basic laboratory workup (CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12) to rule out metabolic causes
Recognize the classic parkinsonian features: asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability
Distinguish idiopathic PD from atypical parkinsonism by noting absence of red flags (no vertical gaze palsy, no cerebellar signs, no severe early autonomic failure)
Initiate appropriate dopaminergic therapy (carbidopa-levodopa) and check for drug interactions given polypharmacy
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing dopamine-blocking agents (haloperidol, metoclopramide) which would worsen parkinsonism
Prescribing anticholinergics in elderly patients with cognitive impairment due to risk of worsening cognition and causing delirium
Abruptly discontinuing dopaminergic medications once started (risk of neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome)
Attributing all symptoms to depression without performing a thorough movement disorder evaluation
Ordering lumbar puncture without clinical indication - not routinely indicated in the workup of parkinsonism
key_reasoning_points: The combination of asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and postural instability in a 68-year-old woman is the classic presentation of idiopathic Parkinson's disease

The patient's depression and cognitive decline (MoCA 22) are important non-motor features of PD but can also be red flags for atypical parkinsonism - the absence of other red flags (no vertical gaze palsy, no cerebellar signs, no severe autonomic failure) supports idiopathic PD
Brain MRI showing non-specific white matter lesions without cerebellar or pontine atrophy rules out MSA and PSP as imaging-supported diagnoses, and the absence of ventriculomegaly rules out NPH

The wide-based gait with stooped posture and positive pull test could raise concern for MSA or PSP, but the presence of prominent resting tremor and the absence of cerebellar ataxia or vertical gaze palsy argues for PD
Borderline orthostatic hypotension must be interpreted in the context of concurrent metoprolol, diabetes, and post-bariatric surgery status before attributing it to autonomic failure from MSA
The patient's medications (fluoxetine, metoprolol) do not include dopamine-blocking agents, effectively ruling out drug-induced parkinsonism
A robust levodopa challenge response (>30% UPDRS improvement) is the strongest supportive criterion for idiopathic PD and distinguishes it from atypical parkinsonism

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic parkinsonian features (asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability) and a normal brain MRI. Despite some features from the source case that could hint at atypical parkinsonism (cognitive decline, autonomic borderline findings, falls), the overall presentation clearly supports idiopathic PD with minimal confounders.
